name: Polynomials, Identities and Factorisation
whatIsIt: A POLYNOMIAL is an expression of terms with whole-number powers, like x² + 3x + 2. IDENTITIES are expansions that are always true: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² and (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b². FACTORISATION is the reverse of expanding — writing an expression as a product: take out a common factor (6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3)) or use an identity (x² − 9 = (x + 3)(x − 3)).
whyNeeded: Identities speed up mental maths (99² = (100−1)² ) and factorisation is the key to solving quadratics and simplifying algebra — the backbone of higher maths, physics and engineering.
realLifeUses: Simplifying formulas by taking out common factors
Areas that split into parts (area model)
Setting up and solving quadratic problems

vocabulary.meaning: An expression of terms with whole-number powers, like x² + 3x + 2.
An equation always true for every value, like (a+b)² = a²+2ab+b².
Write an expression as a product of factors (the reverse of expanding).

story.title: The Quick Square
story.text: Sir asks for 101² without a calculator. Aman uses an identity: (100 + 1)² = 100² + 2×100×1 + 1² = 10000 + 200 + 1 = 10201. 'The identity did the heavy lifting!' Then Sir writes 6x + 9. Aman factors out 3: 3(2x + 3). 'Expanding and factorising are opposites,' Sir smiles.

visual.caption: Area model: 3 × (2x + 3) splits into 3·2x + 3·3 = 6x + 9. Factorising reads it backwards.

teachingGallery.title: Expand and factorise
teachingGallery.note: Distribute to expand; find common factors or identities to factorise.

standardMethod.summary: Expand with the distributive rule or an identity; factorise by taking out common factors or spotting an identity.
steps: Expand a(b + c) = ab + ac; (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²; (a + b)(a − b) = a² − b².
Factorise by taking out the HIGHEST common factor of all terms.
Recognise a difference of squares a² − b² = (a + b)(a − b).
Check by expanding your factors back — you should get the original.
(a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b² with a = x, b = 4.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing (a + b)² = a² + b² (forgetting the middle term 2ab).
commonMistakes.fix: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b². The 2ab middle term is essential — check with numbers: (2+3)² = 25, not 4+9.

commonMistakes.mistake: Taking out a common factor but not the HIGHEST one (6x + 9 → 3(2x + 3) but stopping at a smaller factor).
commonMistakes.fix: Pull out the highest common factor of ALL terms so what's left shares no more factors.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up a quick mental-maths trick using an identity — like squaring 21 as (20+1)² = 400 + 40 + 1 = 441 — and explain how factorising 6x + 9 into 3(2x + 3) is just the reverse of expanding.

revisionCard.summary: Polynomial = terms with whole-number powers. Identities: (a+b)² = a²+2ab+b² (don't drop the 2ab); (a+b)(a−b) = a²−b². Factorise = reverse of expanding: take out the HIGHEST common factor, or spot a difference of squares. Check by expanding back.
